policy_commitment: Achieve net-zero community-wide greenhouse gas emissions for the Austin community by 2040 through direct reductions and, as a last resort, limited offsets/carbon dioxide removal.
source_quote:
Given this, a new goal is being recommended for net-zero community-wide greenhouse gas emissions by 2040, utilizing a steep decline path followed by negative emissions.

Reaching net-zero greenhouse gas emissions means the Austin community will implement the strategies in this plan to reduce our direct emissions as much as possible. We will use carbon offsets or carbon dioxide removal to cancel out any remaining emissions by 2040.
has_measurable_target: Yes — 'net-zero community-wide greenhouse gas emissions by 2040.'
has_legal_mandate: No — stated as the Climate Equity Plan's recommended community goal (plan-level commitment). The text also references the City Council Climate Emergency declaration (Resolution 20190808-078) as motivating context but does not present a city ordinance or legal mandate in the quoted language.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — City of Austin / 'the Austin community' (citywide).
extraction_notes: Clear citywide target: 'Net-zero community-wide greenhouse gas emissions by 2040' is a stated community-level emissions target with a target year and guidance on use of direct reductions and offsets; matches QUANTIFIABLE TARGET and SPECIFIC INTERVENTION criteria.
causal_mechanism: Human activity → ↓ GHG emissions / ↑ carbon removals → ↓ atmospheric forcing
policy_instruments: Direct emissions reductions (decarbonization strategies/community-wide emission cuts); limited use of offsets and carbon dioxide removal (CDR) as last resort to achieve net-zero
classification_signals: "net-zero"; "greenhouse gas emissions"; "negative emissions"; "carbon dioxide removal"; "offsets"; "reduce our direct emissions"

policy_commitment: Electrify transportation so that 40% of total vehicle miles traveled in Austin are provided by electric vehicles by 2030 (targeting ~460,000 EVs), and ensure EV ownership is culturally, geographically, and economically diverse.
source_quote: By 2030, 40% of total vehicle miles traveled in Austin are electrified, and electric vehicle ownership is culturally, geographically, and economically diverse. This translates to approximately 460,000 electric vehicles on the road.
has_measurable_target: Yes — '40% of total vehicle miles traveled in Austin are electrified' by 2030; translates to ~460,000 electric vehicles.
has_legal_mandate: No — plan goal within the Climate Equity Plan; informed by City Council directives but not presented as a law in the quoted language.
extraction_notes: Explicit quantifiable transportation electrification target for vehicle miles traveled (VMT) and an approximate vehicle count; meets QUANTIFIABLE TARGET and SPECIFIC INTERVENTION.
policy_instruments: EV adoption/electrification target; incentives/subsidies for EV purchase; charging infrastructure deployment/expansion; equity-focused outreach and ownership programs; fleet electrification targets
classification_signals: "electrify transportation"; "40% of total vehicle miles traveled electrified"; "electric vehicles"; "460; 000 EVs"; "electric vehicle ownership"
co_benefits_identified: Improved local air quality and public health; reduced oil consumption/imports; potential energy security/resilience benefits; economic development in EV/charging sectors; equity/inclusion outcomes

policy_commitment: Build an equitably distributed EV charging network in Austin sized to accommodate 40% of vehicle miles traveled by 2030 (approximately 226 MW load and >37,000 charging ports), prioritizing systematically excluded areas.
source_quote: By 2030, Austin has a compelling and equitably distributed mix of level 1, 2, and DC fast charging infrastructure to accommodate 40% of total vehicle miles traveled in the city. This translates to 226 megawatts of electrical load and could mean more than 37,000 charging ports.
has_measurable_target: Yes — network sized to accommodate 40% VMT electrification: '226 megawatts of electrical load' and 'more than 37,000 charging ports' (and related mix of Level 1/2/DC fast charging).
has_deadline: Yes — by 2030 (supports the 40% VMT electrification by 2030 target).
has_legal_mandate: No — plan goal/recommendation; implementation actions suggested but not presented as a legal mandate.
extraction_notes: Explicit infrastructure capacity target to support EV adoption tied to the 40% VMT electrification goal (quantified load and port estimate); meets QUANTIFIABLE TARGET and SPECIFIC INTERVENTION.
causal_mechanism: Increase EV charging infrastructure → Increase EV adoption / decrease internal-combustion vehicle use → Decrease fossil fuel combustion → Decrease GHG emissions
policy_instruments: Deployment/build-out of public EV charging infrastructure (level 1; 2; DC fast chargers) sized to meet VMT target; prioritization of deployment in systematically excluded areas (equity-focused siting and distribution); infrastructure planning and capacity provisioning (electrical load sizing)
classification_signals: "EV charging network"; "accommodate 40% of vehicle miles traveled"; "226 MW"; ">37; 000 charging ports"; "level 1; 2; and DC fast charging"; "electrification"; "equitable/equitably distributed"
co_benefits_identified: Improved local air quality and public health; reduced tailpipe pollution/noise; increased transportation equity and access in underserved areas; potential economic development and job creation; potential grid demand flexibility benefits (if paired with smart charging); reduced oil consumption

policy_commitment: By 2030, require/enable net-zero operational carbon for all new buildings, reduce emissions from existing buildings by 25%, and lower natural gas-related emissions by 30% across the Austin community.
source_quote:
By 2030, achieve net-zero carbon* for all new buildings and reduce emissions by 25% for existing buildings while lowering all natural gas-related emissions by 30%.

* For this goal, net-zero carbon implies operational carbon, which refers to the CO2 emitted from operations, such as lighting and heating, during the in-use phase of a building.
has_measurable_target: Yes — 'net-zero carbon for all new buildings' by 2030; 'reduce emissions by 25% for existing buildings' by 2030; 'lowering all natural gas-related emissions by 30%' by 2030.
has_legal_mandate: No — plan goal/target; references Austin Energy plan for generation but the building goals are presented as plan targets to be achieved via codes, incentives, and programs.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — City of Austin / community-wide buildings.
extraction_notes: Explicit building sector target for new and existing buildings and natural gas emissions; meets QUANTIFIABLE TARGET and SPECIFIC INTERVENTION (net-zero for new buildings, percentage reductions for existing buildings and natural gas).
causal_mechanism: Human activity (building operations, natural gas use) → DECREASED GHG emissions (operational carbon) → DECREASED atmospheric forcing
policy_instruments: Building operational carbon standards / codes (net-zero operational carbon requirement); building retrofit programs (energy-efficiency upgrades); electrification of heating (reduce natural gas use); performance-based emissions targets for buildings; incentives/mandates to shift to renewable electricity for building operations
classification_signals: "net-zero operational carbon"; "reduce emissions by 25% for existing buildings"; "lower all natural gas-related emissions by 30%"; "new buildings"; "existing buildings"; "operational carbon (CO2 emitted from operations such as lighting and heating)"

policy_commitment: Austin Energy will pursue a near-term resource plan: 93% carbon-free generation by 2030 and 100% by 2035, with specified conservation and local solar/thermal targets and equity commitments in program participation.
source_quote:
The Austin Energy Resource Plan commits the utility to provide affordable, dependable, and safe electricity service to residents and businesses while pursuing the City's climate change and sustainability goals, including the Austin Climate Emergency Resolution. Austin Energy commits to providing access to the benefits of this 2030 Plan for low-income communities and communities of color.

The plan calls for: - 93% carbon-free generation by 2030, 100% by 2035 - 1,200 megawatts (MW) of conservation, including 225 MW of peak capacity - 1% of retail sales per year in energy efficiency savings, at least 25,000 customer participants annually, 25% limited-income - 375 MW of local solar, 200 MW of customer-sited solar
has_measurable_target: Yes — '93% carbon-free generation by 2030, 100% by 2035; 1,200 MW of conservation (including 225 MW peak); 375 MW local solar; 40 MW local thermal storage; 25% limited-income participation in efficiency.'
has_deadline: Yes — multiple dates (2030 and 2035) specified for targets.
has_legal_mandate: Yes — presented as commitments in the 'Austin Energy Resource, Generation, and Climate Protection Plan to 2030' (utility plan produced by Austin Energy); the plan language is an institutional commitment by Austin Energy.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — Austin Energy's customer load (City of Austin service area).
extraction_notes: Austin Energy's generation and program commitments are stated quantitatively (carbon-free % by year, local solar, conservation MW); qualifies as RESOURCE ALLOCATION / SPECIFIC INTERVENTION by the utility.
causal_mechanism: Human activity → DECREASED GHG emissions (via decarbonized electricity supply and increased renewables/efficiency) → DECREASED atmospheric forcing
policy_instruments: Renewable/carbon-free generation targets; utility resource planning to retire/replace emitting generation; local and customer-sited solar deployment; energy efficiency and conservation programs (demand-side management); program participation targets for low-income customers (equity in program delivery)
classification_signals: "93% carbon-free generation by 2030"; "100% by 2035"; "375 MW of local solar"; "200 MW of customer-sited solar"; "1; 200 megawatts (MW) of conservation"; "1% of retail sales per year in energy efficiency savings"; "conservation"; "energy efficiency savings"
co_benefits_identified: Affordability of electricity; reliability/dependable service; increased access for low-income and marginalized communities; local economic benefits from distributed solar deployment

policy_commitment: Preserve and produce 135,000 housing units by 2027, including 60,000 affordable units, and locate 75% of new housing within 1/2 mile of activity centers and corridors to reduce displacement and support multimodal access.
source_quote:
By 2027, preserve and produce 135,000 housing units, including 60,000 affordable housing units*, with 75% of new housing located within ½ mile of Imagine Austin activity centers and corridors.

*This goal is based on the Council-adopted Austin Strategic Housing Blueprint.
has_measurable_target: Yes — 'By 2027, preserve and produce 135,000 housing units, including 60,000 affordable housing units, with 75% of new housing located within 1/2 mile of Imagine Austin activity centers and corridors.'
has_legal_mandate: No — aligned with the Council-adopted Austin Strategic Housing Blueprint but presented in this plan as a target/goal (implementation may require Council action and funding).
has_geographic_scope: Yes — City of Austin; 75% within 1/2 mile of Imagine Austin activity centers and corridors.
extraction_notes: Housing production and preservation numerical target with a deadline; meets QUANTIFIABLE TARGET and SPATIAL specificity (75% located near activity centers).
causal_mechanism: Same service/access (housing near centers) → decreased travel distance/VMT and more efficient use of transport infrastructure → decreased energy use per capita (and secondary ↓ GHG emissions)
policy_instruments: Transit-oriented development / location-based housing production; affordable housing preservation and production targets; zoning/density changes near corridors and activity centers; incentives for infill development; proximity standards (½-mile to centers)
classification_signals: "preserve and produce 135; 000 housing units"; "60; 000 affordable housing units"; "75% of new housing located within ½ mile of ... activity centers and corridors"; "support multimodal access"; "reduce displacement"
co_benefits_identified: Reduced vehicle miles traveled / lower transport emissions; improved transit ridership and multimodal mobility; reduced household transportation costs; equity and displacement reduction; potential public-health benefits from active transport

policy_commitment: Achieve a 50% mode share for sustainable and shared modes (public transit, biking, walking, carpooling, or avoided trips) across Austin by 2030.
source_quote:
By 2030, 50% of trips in Austin are made using public transit, biking, walking, carpooling, or avoided altogether by working from home*.

*Based on City of Austin and Capital Metro data, includes all trips, and was approximately 20% in 2019.
has_measurable_target: Yes — '50% of trips in Austin are made using public transit, biking, walking, carpooling, or avoided by working from home' (approx. 20% baseline in 2019).
has_legal_mandate: No — plan target; it references City and Capital Metro data and regional plans but is not presented as a binding law in the quoted text.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — City of Austin (all trips in Austin).
extraction_notes: Mode-shift target for trips by 2030 (quantifiable %), a clear mitigation pathway for transport emissions and equity/co-benefits; meets QUANTIFIABLE TARGET and SPECIFIC INTERVENTION.
causal_mechanism: Human activity (transport modal shift) → ↓ GHG emissions
policy_instruments: Modal‑shift target; promotion/expansion of public transit; active‑transport encouragement (walking/biking infrastructure); carpooling incentives; measures to enable avoided trips (telework/trip reduction) and travel demand management
classification_signals: "50% mode share"; "public transit; biking; walking; carpooling; or avoided trips"; "trips"; "By 2030"
co_benefits_identified: Reduced congestion and vehicle kilometers traveled (VKT); improved air quality; public health (active travel); equity/accessibility; reduced road wear and parking demand

policy_commitment: Reduce community-wide greenhouse gas emissions from refrigerant leakage by 25% by 2030 through refrigerant capture/destruction programs, code updates, leak detection incentives, and HVAC training.
source_quote:
By 2030, reduce community-wide greenhouse gas emissions from refrigerant leakage by 25%.

Strategy 1: Capture and destroy old refrigerants - Develop a refrigerant destruction program that places a price on older high ozone-depleting substances and global warming potential (GWP) refrigerants.
has_measurable_target: Yes — 'reduce community-wide greenhouse gas emissions from refrigerant leakage by 25%' by 2030.
has_legal_mandate: No — plan goal with multiple recommended programs and code changes; not stated as a legal mandate in the quoted excerpt.
extraction_notes: Explicit refrigerant leakage reduction target (quantified) for 2030; refrigerant management is a named intervention with strategies; meets QUANTIFIABLE TARGET and SPECIFIC INTERVENTION.
causal_mechanism: Reduced refrigerant leakage / capture & destruction (human activity) → DECREASED GHG emissions → DECREASED atmospheric forcing
policy_instruments: Refrigerant capture-and-destruction program; pricing/taxes or fees on high-GWP/old refrigerants; regulatory code updates for equipment/handling; incentives for leak detection and repair; training/capacity-building for HVAC technicians
classification_signals: "reduce community-wide greenhouse gas emissions"; "refrigerant leakage"; "capture and destroy"; "place a price on ... high ... global warming potential (GWP) refrigerants"; "leak detection incentives"; "HVAC training"; "refrigerant destruction program"
co_benefits_identified: Ozone layer protection (removal of ozone-depleting substances); reduced waste and material loss; lower operating costs for equipment; improved HVAC system performance and reliability; potential public health and safety benefits from proper handling

policy_commitment: Reduce embodied carbon of building materials used in local construction by 40% from a 2020 baseline by 2030 (target: ≤500 kg CO2e/m2 per project) via City design standards, incentives, procurement, and education.
source_quote:
By 2030, reduce the embodied carbon footprint of building materials used in local construction by 40% from a 2020 baseline*.

*The embodied carbon associated with the construction and materials must not exceed 500 kg CO2e/m2 (~100 lbs. CO2e/sf) per project.

policy_commitment: Legally protect an additional 20,000 acres of natural carbon pools and bring total managed natural areas to ~70,000 acres by 2030, prioritizing resilience and benefits for low-income communities and communities of color.
source_quote:
By 2030, legally protect an additional 20,000 acres of carbon pools on natural lands and manage all new and existing natural areas (approximately 70,000 acres total), focusing on resilience.

Strategy 1: Protect natural lands - Identify additional woodland, grassland, and wetland systems for protection, focusing on new conservation lands in the Travis County Eastern Planning Areas while prioritizing benefits for low-income communities and communities of color.
has_measurable_target: Yes — 'legally protect an additional 20,000 acres of carbon pools on natural lands and manage all new and existing natural areas (approximately 70,000 acres total)' by 2030.
has_legal_mandate: No — plan goal that calls for legal protection mechanisms (fee simple, conservation easements) and funding strategies; implementation requires acquisition/commitments (potentially Council action/funding).
has_geographic_scope: Yes — City and surrounding managed natural areas (focus on Travis County Eastern Planning Areas and City-managed lands).
extraction_notes: Natural lands protection and management targets: legal protection acreage (20,000 acres) and total managed acreage (~70,000 acres) by 2030; meets QUANTIFIABLE TARGET and SPECIFIC INTERVENTION (land acquisition, easements, management).

policy_instruments: Legal protection/designation of lands (conservation/protected areas); land acquisition/conservation easements; ecosystem management and restoration of woodlands/grasslands/wetlands; prioritizing equity in siting and benefits

co_benefits_identified: Resilience (reduced climate impacts); benefits to low-income communities and communities of color (equity); biodiversity and habitat protection; flood/stormwater regulation; recreation and amenity values
